title: Your New Pet's First Vet Visit: What to Expect
description: Bringing home a new puppy, kitten, or rescue? Here's what to expect at your first veterinary visit at our NW Portland clinic.
author: Dr. Tracy Mento, DVM
content:
Congratulations on your new family member! Whether you've adopted a puppy, kitten, or adult rescue, one of the first things on your list should be scheduling a veterinary visit. At Forest Heights Veterinary Clinic, we love meeting new patients and helping them get off to a healthy start.

## When to Schedule

Ideally, schedule your first appointment within the first week of bringing your pet home. If your new pet is a young puppy or kitten, they'll need a series of vaccinations over the first few months, so the sooner you start, the better. For adult rescues, an initial exam helps establish a health baseline and catch any issues early.

## What to Bring

To make the most of your first visit, bring:

- **Any medical records** from the breeder, shelter, or previous vet
- **A list of food** your pet is currently eating (brand, type, and amount)
- **Any medications or supplements** your pet is taking
- **A stool sample** (a fresh sample from the same day is ideal for parasite screening)
- **Your questions** — we set aside plenty of time for first visits, so don't hesitate to ask

For cats, bring them in a secure carrier. For dogs, a leash and collar are essential. If your pet is anxious, a light blanket over the carrier can help keep them calm.

## What Happens at the Exam

Your veterinarian will perform a thorough nose-to-tail physical exam, checking:

- **Eyes, ears, and nose** for discharge or abnormalities
- **Mouth and teeth** for dental development, retained baby teeth, or early signs of disease
- **Heart and lungs** via stethoscope
- **Abdomen** by palpation for organ size and any masses
- **Skin and coat** for parasites, fungal infections, or allergies
- **Joints and muscles** for pain, range of motion, and conformation
- **Weight** to establish a baseline for growth tracking

We'll also discuss a vaccination schedule tailored to your pet's age, lifestyle, and risk factors. Core vaccines for dogs include rabies, distemper, and parvovirus. For cats, core vaccines include rabies, feline herpesvirus, calicivirus, and panleukopenia.

## Parasite Prevention

We'll recommend a year-round parasite prevention plan covering fleas, ticks, and intestinal worms. In the Portland area, parasites are active much of the year, so consistent prevention is important even during cooler months.

## Spay or Neuter Timing

If your new pet isn't already spayed or neutered, we'll discuss the ideal timing based on their breed, size, and health. For most dogs and cats, this is typically between 4 and 6 months of age, but larger breed dogs may benefit from waiting longer.

## Getting Comfortable

We know vet visits can be stressful — for pets and their people. Our team takes a gentle, patient approach, and we're happy to go at your pet's pace. Treats are always welcome, and we encourage short "happy visits" where your pet comes in just for some treats and attention with no exam involved.

We're located at 7365 SW Barnes Rd, conveniently serving families in Forest Heights, West Slope, Bethany, Cedar Mill, and the greater NW Portland area. Call (503) 291-1757 to schedule your new pet's first appointment.
